ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for liangfen (mung bean starch jelly). Mapped to mung bean food product as closest defensible ancestor. A more specific term for starch-set jelly dishes does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Starch-based gel / jelly food product (mung bean starch)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for liangfen. A0CKP (starch-based products) used as closest defensible anchor. Exact code for mung bean starch jelly not available in FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for liangfen or mung bean starch jelly as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of liangfen is approximately 200g, representing a standard side-dish or snack portion of the cold jelly dressed with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mung bean starch jelly (closest analogous entry: starch-based jelly / mung bean products)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for liangfen as a dressed dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA mung bean starch data combined with typical dressing components (soy sauce, sesame oil, chili oil, vinegar, garlic). The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) were cross-checked against USDA mung bean starch (FDC ID 174253) and USDA soy sauce (FDC ID 172238) proportional contributions. Energy 85 kcal, protein 0.5g, fat 3.5g (from sesame and chili oil in dressing), carbohydrate 13.5g, sodium 380mg (from soy sauce dressing) are consistent with this composition. Saturated fat estimated at 0.5g based on sesame oil and chili oil fatty acid profiles per USDA. Vitamin D and B12 are confirmed zero for this plant-based dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mung bean starch noodles and starch jelly analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for liangfen (mung bean starch jelly) found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated from the closest analogous food: mung bean starch noodles (bean thread / glass noodles), which have a reported GI of approximately 39–45 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). The cooling process used in liangfen preparation generates resistant starch, which further lowers glycemic response, supporting the low-GI estimate. Glycemic load per 200g serving calculated as: (45 × 13.5 × 2) / 100 = 12.15, rounded to 12.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce dressing (380mg per 100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium soy sauce in the dressing, as a 200g serving contributes approximately 760mg sodium — over 30% of the recommended daily limit of 2000mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and moderate carbohydrate content (13.5g per 100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Liangfen is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; its low GI (~45) and resistant starch content from the cooling process result in a blunted postprandial glucose response, and a 200g serving delivers approximately 27g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~12.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based ingredients, but moderate sodium from dressing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Liangfen is low in saturated fat (~0.5g per 100g) and cholesterol-free, making it broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet, though the sodium content from soy sauce dressing should be monitored and reduced where possible for individuals managing cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Liangfen (凉粉 / 涼粉) is a traditional Chinese cold dish consisting of translucent or white jelly cubes or strips made by boiling mung bean starch (or occasionally pea or sweet potato starch) with water and allowing it to cool and set into a firm, smooth, slippery jelly. The jelly itself is flavourless and neutral, serving as a vehicle for a vibrant, savory-spicy dressing typically composed of soy sauce, rice vinegar, crushed garlic, sesame oil, and chili oil, often garnished with fresh cilantro, sliced scallions, or crushed peanuts. The dish is served cold and is especially popular during summer months across China, with strong regional traditions in Sichuan and Northern China. Nutritionally, liangfen is low in calories (~85 kcal per 100g), very low in sugar (0.5g), low in protein (0.5g), and provides moderate carbohydrate (13.5g) primarily from mung bean starch. The cooling process creates resistant starch, contributing to a low glycemic index of approximately 45. The main nutritional caution is the sodium content (~380mg per 100g) derived from the soy sauce dressing. The dish is vegan, vegetarian, dairy-free, and contains no cholesterol. It is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease in moderate portions, but sodium intake should be monitored for those with hypertension. Common allergens include soy (from soy sauce) and sesame (from sesame oil).
common_user_queries: What is the glycemic index of liangfen?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for liangfen as a dressed dish exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA mung bean starch and dressing component data, cross-checked against the AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9). GI is an analogous estimate from mung bean starch noodles per University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture content for the prepared liangfen dish (jelly plus dressing) is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero as this is an entirely plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; plant foods do not naturally contain vitamin B12 or vitamin D3, confirmed per USDA FoodData Central mung bean starch entry. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this plant-based preparation using unhydrogenated oils. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero as no animal-derived ingredients are present. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical 200g entries) was deduplicated to a single 200g serving. The input source cited (Food Science and Nutrition, onlinelibrary.wiley.com) is not on the approved source list; GI evidence has been re-anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition analogous estimate per approved source policy. Saturated fat (0.5g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.2g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.5g) are estimated from USDA sesame oil and chili oil fatty acid profiles proportional to the fat content of the dish.
